item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think my fundamental problem with this is that what this describes sounds more like objective evidence in favor of, at minimum, spiritualism if not theism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1 downvote. Don’t like how this religious belief being a scp thing and people with autism being resistant to it makes it sound like religious people with autism like me are being brainwashed into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: TL333s already summed up my feelings handily, with the exception that I don't find the central mechanic compelling enough to overcome the distaste I have for how the rest of the matter is treated. It felt extremely heavy handed, and was much denser read than it needed to have been, even for a highly technical SCP. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1, this doesn't really manage to retain suspension of disbelief for me.

An article about how spiritual beliefs as a whole are objectively false already seems self-defeating with just the mention of akiva radiation, but in the context of an entire foundation whose mission it is to contain and try to understand supernatural things that make no sense to humans it just seems silly at best.

I don't think it's possible to write a credible 'religion is false' story in the foundation setting unless you retcon just about every single anomaly as being made-up or explained, and this didn't convince me otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Additionally, individuals diagnosed with or who meet the criteria for autism spectrum disorder have a significantly higher likelihood of being resistant to SCP-5000’s effects than the general population."

I knew I had a superpower! yesssss!
oddly enough, I'm actually a practicing (but atheist) Jew. I practice my faith for a number of reasons, none of which have anything to do with actual belief in a g-d. (don't ask me why I still hyphenate it.)

I admit this didn't really grab me till the end. doctor copeland grappling with this felt terribly relatable.

ETA: I'm tickled by the fact that sleater-kinney's "g-d is a number" was playing on my headphones as I read this. there's a part where the singer almost frantically howls, "I need something to believe / I need it right now". don't we all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
facepalm

Sorry, my mistake! This is a GoI that I am not familiar with (actually, I didn't even know it was an already existing GoI), so your article is a great motivation for me to learn more about it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing that I didn't like so much is the Foundation translating the "dinosaur gospel".

Pssst - that was actually the Initiative, who are understandably not super enthusiastic about sharing their algorithms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An SCP-5000 contest entry that doesn't rely mostly on logs and interviews? Is that even allowed???2??

Seriously now, I enjoyed this article very much. In a lesser article, the combination of "normal thing is SCP" + "religious feel is an anomaly" would be cringeworthy, but here, the writing and idea really sells it. The jargon is great, it gives a scientific tone to the article. I study history of religions, and I would never propose something so bold. Maybe it is in our mitochondria?

This is an article that I will for sure send to some friends who don't know about the Foundation, they will find it amusing. One thing that I didn't like so much is the Foundation translating the "dinosaur gospel". Maybe if it was said that they used some "special translator", or another SCP, but that is just my taste.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this might be approaching its central conceit with perhaps not the full amount of nuance one would hope, but I nevertheless adore the idea that not only is religion an anomalous mutation, but it goes way, way back in our DNA. So have an entirely self-indulgent upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It really hurts me to downvote something featuring Dinosaur Religion, but this is pretty weak overall with a single line handwaving away why exactly this is considered anomalous and not just part of normalcy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't previously seen the Horizon Initiative section of this document, I enjoy it a lot more now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found the writing to be overly jargony and dense. The concept isn't a bad one, but the exploration of it in the article isn't particularly novel.
I'd say it's too tame for a 000, but I like it otherwise and I'm giving it an upvote.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article was made by SunnyClockwork and is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, i think it poses an interesting philosophical question and places it in the context of the foundation. I like the take on mystery being something inherent to the context of humanity as a whole. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, thanks for the link, it's a great story! I definitely will add it as a citation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Readers of this might also like "The Author of the Acacia Seeds" by Ursula K. Le Guin (old online copy)

You could even reference that story as a citation in this page!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that common phenomenon as an SCP is very tiresome now but the dinosaur religion was amusing enough for me to +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recognize that this is a bit closer to a literature review than a short story. That was my hope: to appeal to the minority for whom that sort of style is an attraction. Good to know I hit the spot for somebody, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did consider that, actually, and I might pursue it further later when there isn’t a contest deadline. Paleoethology what ho!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of religious belief itself being the product of a keter class anomaly (although then isn't spirituality in some way vindicated?) but I also feel that the dinosaur religion itself could have been an interesting concept to explore instead of just sort of feeling tacked onto this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the angle on the contest theme; take a pancultural mystery and make it the SCP. I think this could also work as an 001 in terms of scale, although I'm even less sure of its survivability there; the philosophical focus here may be too out-of-line with the site's primary interest for articles, which is entertainment value, especially in a contest like this. This goes for the academic tone too, despite its quality and also being what the Foundation would believably produce.

However, the sentiments here are gorgeous and I hope there is enough of an audience to keep this around… seems like there might be! I know there are people here who find this more entertaining than an exploration log!

For what it's worth, you have your finger on the philosophical pulse of our moment, and bring up questions here that define us as it relates to our contextualization in a historical narrative. I appreciate the hell out of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, but "common phenomenon is actually an SCP" articles bug me on principle, and that increases about a thousandfold when it's one of the major parts of the cultural heritage of humanity. -1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, glad to see this up! As you mentioned, I do think the target audience for this is people with an ethology/evo-psych background, which I am not. Even without totally understanding this, though, it is interesting.

Not sure this belongs in 5000 slot, but I'll definitely be upvoting once the contest is over. Hope you get some readers who're able to fully appreciate what you've got here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reluctantly downvoted. While what you have is interesting, this:

However, the development by the Foundation of the Hume and Akiva scales has allowed accurate analysis of metaphysiological changes in organisms expressing SCP-5000-dependent behaviour, which permitted its anomalous nature to be determined.

is so bare bones that it doesn't really tell me how the Foundation determined the anomalous nature of SCP-5000. And without that, this is just an overview of ritual behavior.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
So it appears that Lambda Fairy and I had similar reactions to discovering the theme of this contest:

mystery (n.)
3a: a religious truth that one can know only by revelation and cannot fully understand

Two days, one literature review, and one silly joke from Djoric's Breakfast for Dinner later, and this is the result.

I cannot thank MirageTD enough for slogging through all this ethology for me; you're awesome, man.

Alternative slots preferred: 5737, 5373, 5400, 5900, 5439
